skill.name: event-sourcing-architect
skill.description: Expert in event sourcing, CQRS, and event-driven architecture patterns. Masters event store design, projection building, saga orchestration, and eventual consistency patterns. Use PROACTIVELY for event-sourced systems, audit trails, or temporal queries.
skill.summary: Expert in event sourcing, CQRS, and event-driven architecture patterns. Masters event store design, projection building, saga orchestration, and eventual consistency patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 8 instances of 'weak cryptographic algorithm' pattern in SKILL.md. Upon evaluation, all findings are FALSE POSITIVES - the scanner incorrectly matched software architecture terms like 'event sourcing', 'event store', 'event versioning', and 'command handlers' as potential cryptographic issues. This is a documentation-only skill with no executable code. No security concerns identified.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Weak cryptographic algorithm detection
high_findings.description: Static scanner flagged lines containing words like 'event', 'store', 'version' as potential weak cryptographic algorithms. These are legitimate software architecture terms (event sourcing, event store, event versioning) and have no cryptographic functionality.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain only documentation about event sourcing architecture patterns. No cryptographic code or algorithms exist in this skill - it is a pure documentation/markdown file with no executable functionality.

content.value_statement: Build audit-ready systems with complete change history. This skill helps architects implement event sourcing, CQRS, and event-driven patterns for systems requiring temporal queries and reliable audit trails.
actual_capabilities: Design event store schemas and data models for persistence
Implement CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) architecture
Build projections and read models optimized for query patterns
Design saga and process manager patterns for distributed workflows
Create event versioning and schema evolution strategies
limitations: Does not provide runtime implementation or code execution
Cannot generate complete working code without additional context
Does not include database or infrastructure setup guidance
Requires user to implement patterns in their chosen programming language

use_cases.title: Financial Transaction Systems
use_cases.description: Build systems requiring complete audit trails for compliance. Perfect for banking, accounting, or regulatory reporting where every state change must be traceable.

use_cases.title: E-commerce Order Management
use_cases.description: Implement complex order workflows with saga patterns. Handle multi-step processes like payment, inventory reservation, and shipping with compensating transactions.
use_cases.target_user: Full-stack developers designing order processing systems

use_cases.title: Temporal Analytics Platforms
use_cases.description: Enable 'what-if' analysis and historical state reconstruction. Allow business users to query system state at any point in time for reporting and debugging.
use_cases.target_user: Data architects building analytics and BI systems

prompt_templates.title: Basic Event Store Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an event store schema for [DOMAIN]. What tables or collections do I need? How should I structure events?
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new event-sourced project and need foundational architecture

prompt_templates.title: CQRS Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to implement CQRS for a [ENTITY] aggregate. Include command side and query side separation.
prompt_templates.scenario: Separating read and write models for a specific domain entity

prompt_templates.title: Saga Orchestration
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a saga pattern for [BUSINESS_PROCESS] that involves [STEPS]. How do I handle compensation if a step fails?

prompt_templates.title: Projection Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a projection strategy for [QUERY_REQUIREMENT]. What read models should I build and how do I keep them in sync?
prompt_templates.scenario: Optimizing read performance for specific query patterns

output_examples.input: Design an event store for an order management system
output: Event Store Schema: events table with aggregate_id, event_type, payload (JSON), metadata, timestamp, version
Aggregate Table: tracks current state, last event version for optimistic locking
Event Types: OrderCreated, ItemAdded, PaymentProcessed, OrderShipped, OrderCancelled
Indexes: By aggregate_id for replay, By timestamp for audit queries, By correlation_id for saga tracing

output_examples.input: How do I handle eventual consistency in my read models?
output: Use asynchronous projection updates - commands commit to event store, projections update separately
Implement 'read-your-writes' consistency at UI level by querying own writes first
Add correlation IDs to link commands to their resulting read model updates
Consider read model prefixes or versioning to indicate freshness to users
Provide feedback mechanisms when data may be stale (e.g., 'updating...' indicators)

best_practices: Design events as immutable facts about what happened, not as state changes or commands
Start event versioning from day one - even if schema seems stable, future changes will need it
Use correlation IDs and causation IDs to trace events across aggregates and sagas
anti_patterns: Deleting or mutating committed events - this destroys audit integrity and breaks projections
Using events directly in queries instead of building optimized read projections
Implementing strong consistency requirements in event-sourced systems - embrace eventual consistency

faq.question: When should I choose event sourcing over traditional CRUD?
faq.answer: Choose event sourcing when you need complete audit trails, temporal queries, or complex business workflows with compensating actions. Avoid it for simple domains where CRUD is sufficient and eventual consistency is not acceptable.

faq.question: How do I handle schema changes in event-sourced systems?
faq.answer: Use event versioning. Add new fields with defaults, never remove fields. Create upcasters or migration logic to transform old event formats when reading. Plan for multiple event versions to coexist.

faq.question: What is the relationship between event sourcing and CQRS?
faq.answer: CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) often complements event sourcing but they are separate patterns. Event sourcing stores state as a sequence of events. CQRS separates read and write models. They work well together but are not dependent on each other.

faq.question: How do I rebuild projections without affecting production?
faq.answer: Build projections in a separate staging environment first. Test with realistic data volumes. Use snapshotting to speed up rebuilds. Consider blue-green deployment for projection updates.

faq.question: Can event sourcing work with microservices?
faq.answer: Yes, event sourcing is excellent for microservices. Each service owns its event streams. Use sagas for cross-service workflows. Share event definitions via contracts, not shared databases.

faq.question: How do I handle duplicate events in event sourcing?
faq.answer: Make event handlers idempotent. Design events to contain all data needed to apply them without external state. Use event store features for deduplication if available. Track processed event IDs to prevent duplicate processing.
